title: Environment, Smearing, Sequence, and Part Preparation
narrationScript: An indication can be lost before the first drop of penetrant goes on. This lesson covers everything surrounding the test. Temperature first. Standard processing holds the part and materials between forty and one hundred twenty-five degrees Fahrenheit. Cold penetrant thickens and fills flaws slowly, while heat dries the film and permanently fades fluorescent dye. Outside the band, you either restore the part to the band or run a procedure qualified at that temperature. Lighting next. Visible red dye wants at least one hundred footcandles of white light at the surface. Fluorescent inspection inverts the rule, a darkened booth at two footcandles or less with at least one thousand microwatts per square centimeter of UV-A making the dye glow. Then surface history. Peening, blasting, sanding, buffing, and aggressive machining smear metal over crack mouths and blind the test, and a controlled etch reopens them. Hence the sequence rule. Inspect after every operation that can create or expose a flaw, before anything that smears, seals, or coats the surface. Strip coatings, clean, etch when required, dry, then test.

sections.heading: The Temperature Window and Its Edges
sections.body:
Standard practice holds the part, the penetrant, and everything that touches them between **40 and 125 °F (4 and 52 °C)** during processing. The band exists because temperature changes the liquid you are working with - and the surface it is working on.

At the **cold end**, viscosity climbs and capillary filling slows, so procedures extend dwell as parts approach 40 °F. Below the band, stop: water-washable penetrants, hydrophilic removers, and aqueous developers can freeze or behave unpredictably, and a cold part sweats - condensation sitting in a flaw blocks penetrant entry as surely as oil. The cures are to bring part and materials into the band, or to run a procedure **specifically qualified at the nonstandard temperature** - typically demonstrated by processing cracked comparator blocks at both the standard and the proposed temperature and comparing the indications.

At the **hot end**, the penetrant's volatile constituents flash off; the film thickens, dries, and stops feeding the flaws. Worse, fluorescent dyes suffer **heat fade** - brightness lost to overheating does not come back. That is why dryers are held to about **160 °F part temperature** maximum, and why a part fresh from a dryer, a weld, or a hot process must cool back into the band before penetrant touches it.

sections.heading: Lighting From White to Ultraviolet
sections.body:
PT delivers its verdict to the human eye, so lighting is a process variable with the same standing as dwell time - measured with a calibrated meter, at the part surface, and recorded.

**Visible (Type II)** inspection lives on **color contrast** - red dye against white developer - and wants the surface flooded with white light: commonly a minimum of **100 fc (≈1,000 lux)** at the examination surface. Intensity falls off rapidly with distance, so the meter goes where the part sits, not where the fixture looks bright.

**Fluorescent (Type I)** inspection inverts the rule. The dye absorbs **UV-A** (320-400 nm, peaking near 365 nm) and re-emits **yellow-green light near 550 nm**, close to the eye's peak response. The indication's glow is fixed by the dye and the UV-A dose, so contrast is controlled by *darkness*: at least **1,000 µW/cm² of UV-A** at the examination surface and **no more than 2 fc** of ambient white light in the booth. Every footcandle of white leakage - a propped door, a cracked lamp filter, fluorescing paper or clothing - raises the background and washes indications out.

The inspector is part of the system: dark-adapt **at least 1 minute** per ASTM E1417 - codes such as ASME Section V, Article 6 require 5 - before evaluating; let mercury-vapor lamps warm to stable output before metering or inspecting; and never examine through **photochromic or permanently tinted lenses**. LED UV-A lamps are qualified and checked under **ASTM E3022** where the contract invokes it.

sections.caption: Visible PT floods the surface with white light; fluorescent PT starves the booth of it and lets UV-A make the dye glow - two routes to the same goal, maximum contrast.

sections.heading: Metal Smearing: Flaws Sealed by Surface Work
sections.body:
Metal at a worked surface does not just abrade away - it **flows**. Peening media, blast grit, abrasive paper, wire bristles, buffing wheels, and dull cutting tools all plastically smear a thin, ductile layer across the surface, and that layer folds over crack mouths like pie crust over a knife slit. A smeared lip only **a few ten-thousandths of an inch** thick seals the opening completely - and a sealed flaw produces no indication, no matter how well the rest of the process is run.

Treat these operations as smear suspects whenever they touch a surface bound for PT: **shot peening, abrasive, grit, or vapor blasting, plastic media blasting, tumbling, wire brushing, sanding, buffing and polishing, lapping, honing, grinding, and aggressive machining**. Soft alloys - aluminum and magnesium - smear most readily, but titanium, nickel alloys, and steel are not immune.

Here is the trap: a grit-blasted part arrives looking *better* than it ever has - bright, uniform, clean. The Level II habit is to read the **traveler**, not the shine. If a smearing operation precedes the inspection, the test may be blind until the surface is chemically etched - and 'it looked fine' is not a defense an audit accepts.

sections.caption: Surface work folds metal over the crack mouth and blinds PT; a controlled etch dissolves the smeared layer and restores capillary entry.

sections.heading: Etching: The Fix and Its Side Effects
sections.body:
The repair for a smeared surface is a **controlled chemical etch**: an acid or alkaline solution dissolves the thin layer of displaced metal and reopens the flaw mouths beneath it. *Controlled* is the operative word - the etchant, the immersion time, and the amount of metal removed are set by the governing specification and the **cognizant engineering organization**, never improvised at the tank. Whether an etch is required at all is likewise an engineering call: practices such as ASTM E1417 require etching before penetrant examination **when the contract or specification invokes it**.

Etching has side effects a Level II must manage:

- **Residues kill fluorescence.** Acid and chromate residues left on the surface or in flaws degrade fluorescent dye brightness - so neutralize, rinse thoroughly, and dry completely before penetrant goes on.
- **Hydrogen embrittlement.** Acids charge high-strength steels with hydrogen; specifications may require a **hydrogen-relief bake** after etching. Your procedure governs.
- **Etching undoes peening.** Shot peening earns its keep by leaving a thin compressive surface layer - and an etch dissolves exactly that layer. You do not 'peen now, etch and inspect later': the sound sequence is **penetrant inspection before peening**, and any post-peen etch needs engineering disposition.

sections.title: The sequence rule, in one breath
sections.body: Final penetrant inspection comes **after** the last operation that can create or expose a surface discontinuity - machining, grinding, welding, straightening, heat treating - and **before** any operation that can smear, close, or cover one - peening, blasting, buffing, plating, anodizing, painting. When a smearing operation must precede the test, the surface is **etched first where the specification requires it**, and coated parts are inspected **after stripping**, never through the coating. Some welding codes add a hold - up to 48 hours on crack-sensitive high-strength steel welds - so delayed cracking has time to appear before the final inspection.

sections.heading: Part Preparation in Practice: Stripping, Cleaning, Drying
sections.body:
For new manufacture, sequencing is mostly scheduling. For in-service hardware, **you** create the inspectable surface - and the order matters.

- **Strip coatings first.** Penetrant cannot interrogate metal through paint, primer, anodize, or plating - the coating bridges and seals the openings, so inspection through a coating happens only when the engineering authority explicitly allows it. Chemical stripping is preferred for PT preparation precisely because it does not deform metal; mechanical removal - plastic media blast, abrasive blast, sanding - smears, and specifications commonly require an **etch after mechanical stripping** before penetrant is applied.
- **Match the cleaning to the contaminant.** Solvent wipe or vapor degrease for oils and greases; alkaline or ultrasonic cleaning for heavy or batched soils; acid pickling for rust and scale. The Level I rules still stand - the Level II adds the *why*: anything left in the flaw cavity either blocks entry or dilutes and degrades the penetrant that gets in.
- **Dry like it matters.** Water or solvent sitting in a flaw is invisible sabotage. Dry per procedure - typically warm circulating air with **part temperature at or below about 160 °F** - then let the part cool into the 40-125 °F band before penetrant application.

Last, read the part's history before you process it. The router tells you what touched this surface - and whether you are about to inspect a part, or merely test its smeared shell.

sections.caption: Prior operations and the correct PT response - typical practice; the governing procedure and engineering authority rule.
rows: Can create cracks - quench, delayed, or strain-induced
Final PT after these operations, including any code-required hold on crack-sensitive welds
Can create tears and grinding cracks - and smear flaw mouths shut
PT after the operation; etch first when smearing is suspected or specified
Plastic flow seals surface openings; a later etch would strip the compressive layer
PT before peening - never plan on etching afterward
Abrasive, grit, or vapor blasting; wire brushing; sanding; buffing
Smears metal over flaw mouths even while it 'cleans'
Avoid before PT, or follow with the specified etch
Coating bridges and seals surface openings
PT before coating; strip - chemically where possible - before in-service PT
Reopens smeared flaws but leaves residues and can charge steel with hydrogen
Neutralize, rinse, and dry before penetrant; hydrogen-relief bake when required

figures.title: Two lighting regimes: visible vs fluorescent
Smeared metal and the etch that reopens it
Process routing: where final PT belongs

interactive.title: Place the Final Penetrant Inspection
interactive.intro: A high-strength steel landing-gear link follows the routing below. Click the numbered point where the final fluorescent penetrant inspection belongs.

regions.label: Point 1 - after rough machining
regions.feedback: Too early for the final inspection. Heat treatment can quench-crack the part, and finish machining and grinding can crack or smear the surface after this point. An in-process check here can be useful - the final PT cannot be.

regions.label: Point 2 - after the etch, before shot peen
regions.feedback: Correct. Every flaw-producing operation - machining, heat treat, grinding - is behind you, the etch has reopened anything smearing closed, and the surface is still bare. Inspect now, then peen and coat.

regions.label: Point 3 - after shot peening
regions.feedback: Too late. Peening has already folded metal over any crack mouths, and etching now would dissolve the compressive layer the peen was applied to create. PT belongs before the peen.

regions.label: Point 4 - after anodize and paint
regions.feedback: Too late. Coatings bridge and seal surface openings - penetrant cannot interrogate the metal through them. Inspecting here would mean stripping the brand-new coating.

checkQuestions.question: A water-washable fluorescent inspection is scheduled on parts stored overnight in an unheated trailer; part temperature reads 34 °F. What does standard practice require?
options: Proceed, but double the penetrant dwell to compensate for the cold
Bring the part into the standard 40-125 °F band - or use a procedure specifically qualified at the lower temperature - before applying penetrant
Switch to a visible-dye penetrant, which is unaffected by temperature
Apply the penetrant cold and warm the part during dwell with a torch
checkQuestions.explanation: Below 40 °F the process is outside its qualified range: viscosity is up, aqueous materials can freeze, and condensation can wet the surface. Dwell extension alone does not requalify the process, dye type does not change the physics, and uncontrolled torch heating risks overshooting the band and drying the film. Warm the part properly, or run a procedure qualified at temperature - typically demonstrated on cracked comparator blocks.

checkQuestions.question: A fluorescent booth checks out at 1,200 µW/cm² of UV-A, but a propped door lets ambient white light reach 10 fc at the part, and indications look washed out. Why?
options: White light chemically destroys the fluorescent dye in the flaw
Ambient white light reduces the UV-A output of the lamp
The developer only blots penetrant out of flaws in darkness
An indication's glow is fixed, so raising the background brightness collapses the contrast ratio and the eye can no longer pick the indication out
checkQuestions.explanation: Fluorescent PT is a brightness-contrast method: the glow is set by the dye and the UV-A dose, so visibility depends on a near-black background. At 10 fc the background luminance swamps the fixed indication brightness - which is why practices cap booth ambient light at about 2 fc. The dye is not destroyed and lamp output is unchanged; close the door and dark-adapt again before evaluating.

checkQuestions.question: Machined steel fittings arrive for PT 'pre-cleaned' by grit blasting, and they look spotless. What is the Level II concern?
options: Blasting plastically flows surface metal over crack mouths - the parts may be sealed against penetrant entry and need the specified etch before testing
Grit blasting work-hardens the surface so penetrant cannot wet it
Blasting residue fluoresces and will produce false linear indications
The roughened surface holds too little penetrant for indications to form
checkQuestions.explanation: Blasting plastically deforms the surface; displaced metal folds over crack mouths and seals them against capillary entry even as the part looks cleaner than ever. When smearing operations precede PT, the specification commonly requires a controlled chemical etch to remove the smeared layer before testing. Wetting, fluorescing grit, and surface 'capacity' are not the failure mechanisms.

checkQuestions.question: A router for a high-strength steel link reads: finish grind, then shot peen, then fluorescent PT, then paint. What should a Level II flag?
options: Nothing - PT falls after grinding and before paint, which satisfies sequencing
PT should move after painting so the inspection covers the final surface
PT must move ahead of shot peening - peening smears metal over crack mouths, and etching after peening would strip the compressive layer it was applied to create
Shot peening must always be performed after painting
checkQuestions.explanation: Peening seals surface openings by plastic flow, so a crack would be invisible to the PT that follows it. Etching after peening is not the answer either - it dissolves the thin compressive layer peening exists to create. The inspection moves ahead of the peen: after grinding (and any required etch), before peening and paint.

checkQuestions.question: After an acid etch to remove smeared metal, which preparation step protects the fluorescent inspection that follows?
options: Apply developer immediately so it absorbs any leftover acid
Neutralize and rinse the etchant completely, then dry the part - acid and chromate residues degrade fluorescent dye brightness
Apply penetrant while the surface is still wet so the etchant carries it into the flaws
Heat the part well above the dryer limit to burn off the residues
checkQuestions.explanation: Acid and chromate residues left on the surface or in flaws degrade fluorescent dye brightness, and any liquid trapped in a flaw blocks or dilutes penetrant entry. The etched part is neutralized, rinsed, and dried, then processed. Penetrant on a wet surface and 'burn-off' above the dryer limit both destroy the inspection, and developer is not a cleanup tool.

references: CP-105-2024 PT Level II Topical Outline §3.1.6-3.1.9 (temperature and lighting, metal smearing, sequence, part preparation)
ASTM E1417/E1417M, liquid penetrant testing practice (temperature band, lighting minimums, sequencing and etching requirements)
ASTM E165/E165M, liquid penetrant examination practice (general reference)
ASTM E3022, LED UV-A lamp practice (fluorescent examination lighting)
